text: And it's known as what Michael Feathers describes as the legacy code change algorithm.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And there's an algorithm that he lays out anytime you need to change anything in a legacy code base.

text: And it's basically a five-step process.

text: And what I want to do in today's podcast is talk about the legacy code change algorithm and what it means in each step.

text: Step number two, find test points.

text: Step number three, break dependencies.

text: Step number four, write tests.

text: Step number five, which is the final step, make changes and refactor.

text: So as you might know, the purpose of working with a legacy code is because we have to change the code.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: They're like safety pads that I have laid out on the ground for myself in case I fall down, which I'm breaking the code.

text: This test basically is just wrapping the entire thing.

text: It may take a long time to execute, but at least I can verify the behavior.

text: I didn't break anything.

text: So if I'm going to write tests for new code, well, I'm already going to perhaps be breaking my dependencies apart.

text: I'll be relying on interfaces or some type of thing that allows me to break these dependencies very easily so I can write unit tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so you have to figure out how you're going to break these dependencies apart.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so, for example, one way that I have done this many times is inside of a method, I would see that a class was being instantiated.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So maybe it's, you know, my database layer and say my database layer, you know, DB equals new database layer.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's literally a concrete implementation right there inside of the method.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Then back in my other class that I kind of need to, I'm trying to wrap a test around, I might go up to a constructor.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and then rely on the interface.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I might have a field, say, hey, here's the interface, be called maybe my database layer interface.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'm just going to use some random name here.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then inside of the constructor, when the constructor instantiates, I would pass in a new instance of the database layer.

text: However,

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It would allow me to test without having an actual database up and running.

text: Well, what you can also do is you can create what's also known as a gateway type pattern.

text: And there's a couple of names for this.

text: It could be called a facade, could be whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I like to call it a gateway.

text: And what a gateway is, is let's say that you're using a class and it has a bunch of static methods on it, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it's from another third party open source library and it just has static methods.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You call on it and it does something.

text: Those methods may go out to the network.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So what I'll do is actually create another class and I'll call this my, you know, my database layer gateway.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Let's say that was, you know, we had a Sarah database layer was all static calls.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And this class, my database layer gateway has an interface on it that basically has the same exact methods.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And what I'm really doing is just creating a wrapper.

text: That's all I'm doing, wrapping the existing static gateway and kind of replacing that code in there.

text: It's still calling the same exact code, but now I am kind of wrapping it around an abstraction that I can then later provide a fake or a mock for to allow me to do that.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Continually update.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Continually write new tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Don't bite off more than you can chew.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This will come out to bite you in the rear end real fast.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that is the basic fundamental legacy code change algorithm to recap the steps here.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Number one, you're going to identify the change points.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: These are where you need to make changes.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Number two, you're going to find the test points, which are where you're going to actually build and what you need to test.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Number three, you need to break those dependencies because most likely in that legacy code, there's a bunch of dependent stuff and you need to figure out how to break that stuff apart.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Number four, you're going to write the tests that verify the current behavior of the application.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So then you can verify that you didn't really break anything.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And finally, step five, you're going to make changes and refactor.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This is where you're going to write the new code, where you're going to change the existing code, and then you're going to write some more tests to verify the new behavior as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that's the legacy code change algorithm.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's kind of the basis for a lot of what you're going to see in working effectively with legacy code.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Again, I'm going to talk about all different types of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This is one of my favorite books on software development in general.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The book actually, I think the examples are all in Java.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And when I read this, I was not a Java developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I was a PHP and .NET developer and Rails developer before I'd even got into Android and Java is when I read this.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it doesn't matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Don't let the language off put you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if you're a Python developer and you're listening to this and you think like, I don't want to read Java.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Don't worry what language it's written in.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: All of these concepts apply across the board.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're going to run into legacy code all over the place.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It doesn't matter if it's Python or Java or Kotlin or Swift or whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're going to run into legacy code in your career.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And knowing how to work with that legacy code,
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: is a superpower.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'm not going to lie.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're going to walk in.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're going to understand these steps, the legacy code change algorithm, and the things that we'll be talking about of how to break these things apart inside of the legacy application is going to help you grow in your career immensely.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So stay tuned.
